questions.text: What is the normal arterial pH range?
Which best describes the relationship between pH and [H⁺]?

answers.text: Linear and direct — they rise and fall together
Inverse — pH is the negative log of free [H⁺]

questions.text: By definition, an acid is a substance that:
A blood pH greater than 7.45 is described as:
Which three systems work together to maintain blood pH?
The primary role of blood buffers is to:

answers.text: Replace bicarbonate produced by the kidneys
Prevent large changes in pH when the blood is exposed to acid–base abnormalities

questions.text: Which factor determines the QUANTITY of CO₂ produced and entering the blood?
Which factor determines the quantity of CO₂ ELIMINATED from the blood?
Compared to fat metabolism, carbohydrate metabolism produces:
The cellular organelle known as the cell's 'powerhouse,' where the Krebs cycle generates ATP, is the:
The body's PREFERRED source of short-term energy is:
Carbohydrate is stored in the liver and muscle primarily as:
Which macronutrient is used primarily for growth and repair?
Which macronutrient provides long-term energy and contains the most energy per gram?
What is the O₂ : CO₂ ratio for CARBOHYDRATE metabolism?
What is the O₂ : CO₂ ratio for LIPID / FAT metabolism?
What is the O₂ : CO₂ ratio for PROTEIN metabolism?
The respiratory quotient (RQ) is defined as:
An RQ greater than 1.0 most likely suggests:

answers.text: Underfeeding and use of ketones as fuel
Excessive carbohydrate or calorie provision that increases CO₂ production and may cause difficulty weaning from mechanical ventilation

questions.text: An RQ less than 0.7 most likely suggests:

answers.text: Underfeeding and use of ketones as a fuel source

questions.text: Which pulmonary disorder is NOT specifically listed in the lesson as being affected by nutrition?
An increase in alveolar ventilation will cause PaCO₂ to:
What is the normal arterial PCO₂?
Which form of CO₂ accounts for approximately 80% of CO₂ transport from tissues to the lungs?
Approximately what percentage of oxygen is bound to hemoglobin in arterial blood?
Approximately what percentage of CO₂ is carried bound to hemoglobin (carbamino compounds)?
The major intracellular blood buffer is:

answers.text: Lipid bilayer of the cell membrane

questions.text: A LOW pH (acidosis) shifts the oxyhemoglobin curve to the RIGHT. This means hemoglobin's affinity for O₂:

answers.text: Decreases — Hb gives up O₂ more easily

questions.text: An INCREASE in body temperature shifts the oxyhemoglobin curve in which direction and with what effect on Hb affinity?
HIGH levels of 2,3-DPG in red blood cells:

answers.text: Shift the curve LEFT and increase Hb affinity for O₂
Shift the curve RIGHT and decrease Hb affinity for O₂, promoting O₂ unloading at the tissues
Have no effect on Hb affinity

questions.text: Which effect describes how O₂ binding influences hemoglobin's ability to carry CO₂?
Which effect describes how CO₂, H⁺, and temperature influence hemoglobin's affinity for O₂?
Which term describes the abnormal STATE OF THE BLOOD when pH is below normal?
In the ROME mnemonic, the 'O' stands for and reminds you that:

answers.text: Opposite — PaCO₂ moves OPPOSITE to pH (respiratory disorders)
Origin — bicarbonate originates from the kidneys

questions.text: In the ROME mnemonic, the 'E' stands for and reminds you that:

answers.text: Equal — HCO₃⁻ moves in the SAME (equal) direction as pH (metabolic disorders)

questions.text: What is the normal HCO₃⁻ range?
An ABG shows pH 7.25, PaCO₂ 60 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 24 mEq/L. The acid–base disturbance is:
An ABG shows pH 7.52, PaCO₂ 28 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 24 mEq/L. The acid–base disturbance is:
An ABG shows pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 38 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 14 mEq/L. The acid–base disturbance is:
An ABG shows pH 7.50, PaCO₂ 40 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 32 mEq/L. The acid–base disturbance is:
Which of the following is a typical cause of RESPIRATORY ACIDOSIS?

answers.text: Drug or sedation overdose causing hypoventilation

questions.text: Which of the following is a typical cause of RESPIRATORY ALKALOSIS?
Which of the following is a typical cause of METABOLIC ACIDOSIS?
Which of the following is a typical cause of METABOLIC ALKALOSIS?

answers.text: Severe vomiting and N-G tube suctioning

questions.text: Hypoxemia and hypoxia differ in that:

answers.text: Hypoxemia is low oxygen in the BLOOD, while hypoxia is deficient oxygen reaching the TISSUES (global or local)
Hypoxemia and hypoxia mean the same thing
Hypoxia is always reversible; hypoxemia is not
Hypoxemia only occurs in pediatric patients
